chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
subtopic: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Einstein's Photoelectric Equation: Energy Quantum of Radiation
summary: Given: Energy of photon, E = 2.20 eV Work function of, $ \mathrm{Cs}_{\phi}=2.14\ \mathrm{eV} $, $$ \mathrm{K}_{\phi}=2.30~\mathrm{e V}, $$ $$ \mathrm{Na}_{\phi}=2.75\mathrm{eV} $$ we know.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - The energy of a photon with wavelength $ \lambda $ is given by $ e = \frac{hc}{\lambda} $. For photoelectric effect, the relation for maximum kinetic energy of the ejected electron is, $$ \mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=e-\boldsymbol{\phi} $$ - Given: Energy of photon, E = 2.20 eV Work function of, $ \mathrm{Cs}_{\phi}=2.14\ \mathrm{eV} $, $$ \mathrm{K}_{\phi}=2.30~\mathrm{e V}, $$ $$ \mathrm{Na}_{\phi}=2.75\mathrm{eV} $$ we know. electron emits when $ h\nu > \phi_{0} $ So, it is clear that energy of photon is more than the work function of Cs only. Hence, Cs can emit the photoelectrons.
##### Related Theory The number of photoelectrons emitted per second is proportional to the intensity of incident radiation.
##### Mnemonics Electron emission can be done by 3 process (1) The-Thermionic Emission (2) Field-Field Emission (3) Photo-Photoelectric Emission - According to photoelectric effect, $$ h\mathbf{v}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+\frac{e\mathbf{V}_{s}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{E}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+\mathbf{e}\mathbf{V}_{o} $$ $$ \frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}+e\mathbf{V}_{s} $$ $$ \frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2}=\frac{-e\mathbf{V}_{s}}{2} $$ $$ -h\mathbf{v}=e\mathbf{V}_{s} $$ Putting (iii) in eq (i), we get $$ h\mathbf{v}=h\mathbf{v}_{o}-\frac{h\mathbf{v}}{2} $$ So, $$ \mathbf{v}_{o}=\frac{3}{2}\mathbf{v} $$ - Work function of material is $$ \mathbf{W}=h\mathbf{v}=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$ Here h = planck's constant $$ h=6.63\times10^{-34}\mathrm{J{-}s} $$ $$ c=\mathrm{speed~of~light}=3\times10^{8}\mathrm{~m/s} $$ $$ \lambda=wavelength~of~light $$ And $$ \mathbf{W}=h\mathbf{v}=4\times1.6\times10^{-19}\mathbf{J} $$ From eq. (i) and (ii) we can write, $$ 4\times1.6\times10^{-19}=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{\lambda} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{4\times1.6\times10^{-19}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=3.108\times10^{-7}m\\&=310nm\end{aligned} $$ - From Einstein's photoelectric equation, we can write, $$ \mathrm{E}=\mathbf{W}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ where $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=\mathbf{W}or\mathbf{k}\;function $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}=Kinectic energy $$ and $$ \mathbf{E}=h\mathbf{v} $$ When incident frequency of light is $ v = 2v_{0} $, then eq. (i) become, $$ h(2\mathbf{v}_{0})=h\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2} $$ $$ h\mathbf{v}_{0}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2} $$ When incident frequency of light is $ v = 5v_{0} $, then eq. (i) becomes, $$ \begin{aligned}h(5\mathbf{v}_{0})&=h\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}\\4h\mathbf{v}_{0}&=\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ From eq. (ii) and eq. (iii), we have $$ \begin{aligned}&\frac{h\mathbf{v}_{0}}{4h\mathbf{v}_{0}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}}\\ \Rightarrow\quad&\frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}^{2}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}\\ \Rightarrow\quad&\frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=1:2\\ \end{aligned} $$ - Given: Threshold wavelength, $ \lambda_{0} = 3250 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} $ Wavelength of UV light, $ \lambda = 2536 \times 10^{-10} $ m We know that Einstein's photoelectric equation is. $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{K}+\mathrm{W}\\&\mathrm{h}\mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}+h\mathrm{v}_{0}\\ \Rightarrow\ \frac{1}{2}m\mathrm{v}^{2}=h\mathrm{v}-h\mathrm{v}_{0}=h(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}_{0})\\&=\quad h c\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)\\ \Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{v}&=\sqrt{\frac{2h c}{m_{0}}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\right)}\\&=\sqrt{\frac{2\times6.02\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{9.1\times10^{-31}}\left(\frac{3250-2536}{3250\times2536}\right)}\\&=0.6\times10^{6}\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\\&=6\times10^{5}\mathrm{~m s}^{-1}\end{aligned} $$ - From photoelectric equation, we have $$ h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}_{0}^{2} $$ where, $$ KE=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W} $$ putting the given values. $$ 2eV=5eV-W $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=3\mathbf{V} $$ $$ V_{\text{cathode}} - V_{\text{anode}} = 3\,V $$ $$ \therefore\mathrm{V}_{\text{anode}}-\mathrm{V}_{\text{cathode}}=-3\mathrm{~V} $$ - From Einstein's photoelectric equation, where, $$ \begin{array}{r}{h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}}\\ {\mathbf{W}=\mathrm{w o r k f u n c t i o n}}\\ {\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=\mathrm{k i n e t i c e n e r g y}}\\ {\mathbf{K}_{\mathrm{m a x}}=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W}}\end{array} $$ $$ \mathbf{K}_{\max}=\frac{hc}{\lambda}-\mathbf{W} $$ In the question, maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is 3 times that in first case. $$ 3K_{\max}=\frac{hc}{\frac{\lambda}{2}}-W $$ From eq. (i) and (ii), $$ \mathbf{W}=\frac{hc}{2\lambda} $$ - From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+(\mathbf{K}.\mathbf{E}.)_{\max} $$ $$ \left(\mathbf{K.E.}\right)_{\max}=h\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{W} $$ In first condition, $$ 0.5=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W} $$ In second condition, $$ 0.8=1.2\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W} $$ From (ii) and (iii), $$ -0.3=-0.2\mathrm{E} $$ $$ E=\frac{0.3}{0.2}=1.5eV $$ Putting in eq. (ii) we get, $$ \begin{aligned}&0.5=1.5-\mathbf{W}\\&\mathbf{W}=1.5-0.5=1\mathbf{eV}\\ \end{aligned} $$ - We know that, $$ \frac{1}{2}m v_{\mathrm{max}}^{2}=h v $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\max}^{2}=\frac{2h\mathbf{v}}{m} $$ $$ \mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{max}}=\sqrt{\frac{2h\mathbf{v}}{m}} $$ - $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{v}_{\max}&=\sqrt{\frac{2}{m}(\mathrm{KE}_{\max})}\\ &=\sqrt{\frac{2}{m}(\mathrm{E}_{ph}-\mathrm{W})}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \frac{\mathbf{v}_{1}}{\mathbf{v}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1-0.5}{2.5-0.5}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.5}{2}}=1:2 $$ - Given. Threshold frequency, $ v_{0} = 3.3 \times 10^{14} $ Hz and light of frequency, $ v = 8.2 \times 10^{14} $ Hz Then we know from Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ \mathbf{e V}_{0}=\mathbf{E}-h\mathbf{v}_{0} $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{E}-h\mathbf{v}_{0}}{e} $$ $$ \mathbf{V}_{0}=\frac{h(\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{v}_{0})}{e} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{6.62\times10^{-34}(8.2\times10^{14}-3.3\times10^{14})}{1.6\times10^{-19}} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{6.62\times10^{-34}}{1.6}\times4.9\times10^{33} $$ $$ V_{0}=\frac{6.62\times4.9\times10^{-1}}{1.6} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=2\mathrm{v o l t} $$ - Using Einstein's photoelectric equation where. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{KE}_{\max}&=\mathrm{E}-f\\f&=\text{work function}\\\mathrm{KE}_{\max}&=\text{Maximum kinetic energy}\\h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}+f\\h\mathbf{v}&=5\mathrm{eV}+6.2\mathrm{eV}=11.2\mathrm{eV}\\\lambda&=\left(\frac{12400}{112\mathrm{eV}}\right)=1.104\times10^{-7}\mathrm{n}\end{aligned} $$ Hence, the radiation lies in ultraviolet region. - According to given situation, $$ \begin{aligned}&and\\ &\Longrightarrow\\ \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{E}_{0}+\mathbf{K}\\2h\mathbf{v}&=\mathbf{E}_{0}+\mathbf{K}^{\prime}\\\mathbf{K}^{\prime}&=\mathbf{K}+h\mathbf{v}\end{aligned} $$

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Experimental Study of Photoelectric Effect
summary: Photocell employs photoelectric effect to convert change in the intensity of illumination into a change in photoelectric current.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Stopping potential is independent of distance, hence, new stopping potential will remain unchanged. Stopping potential = $ V_{0} $ - In a photoelectric effect, Stopping potential = Maximum kinetic energy So, $$ e\mathbf{V}=\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}_{\max} $$ $$ V=\frac{KE_{max}}{e}=\frac{0.5eV}{e}=0.5V $$ - From Einstein's photoelectric equation, $$ \mathbf{E}=h(\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{v}_{0}) $$ $$ \mathsf{v}\ge\mathsf{v}_{0} $$ - Kinetic energy of photoelectrons depends on the frequency of incident radiation and number of photoelectrons depend on the intensity. - We know that energy of incident light $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=6.2\mathrm{eV} $$ Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=\mathrm{W}+\mathrm{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}\\\mathrm{e}\mathrm{V}_{0}&=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W}\\&=(6.2-5.01)\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}&=1.2\ eV\\ \mathbf{V}_{0}&=1.2\ V\end{aligned} $$ - Curves $a$ and $b$ represent incident radiations of same frequency but of different intensities. - The number of photoelectrons emitted is directly proportional to the intensity of light. - Intensity is given by $$ I=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi d^{2}} $$ where, P = Power of source d = distance We know (assume) that light to spread out uniformly in all direction, i.e., spherical source. $$ I\propto\frac{1}{d^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{d_{2}^{2}}{d_{1}^{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=4 $$ $$ I_{2}=\frac{I_{1}}{4} $$ - Photocell employs photoelectric effect to convert change in the intensity of illumination into a change in photoelectric current.
##### Related Theory A photocell is a technological application of the photoelectric effect. It is a device which converts light signal into an electrical signal. - For a point source, $ 1 \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}} $ As number of photoelectrons $ \propto $ intensity, so, number of photoelectrons is quarter of the initial number. - Einstein's photoelectric effect theory states that a single incident photon ejects a single electron. As a result, as the intensity increases, the number of incident photons increases, and the number of emitted electrons increases, the photo current increases. - By work function of a metal, it means that the minimum energy required for the electron in the highest level of conduction band to get out of the metal. The work function has no effect on photoelectric current as long as $ h\nu > W_{0} $. The photoelectric current is proportional to the intensity of incident light. Since, there is no change in the intensity of light. Hence, $$ I_{1}=I_{2} $$ - When a photon of light with frequency v strikes a photosensitive metal surface, its energy (hv) is consumed in two ways. A portion of the photon's energy is utilized to liberate the electron from the metal surface, which is equal to the metal's work function $ W_{0} $. $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=h\mathbf{v}_{0} $$ (where, $ v_{0} $ is threshold frequency) $$ \Rightarrow\quad\mathbf{w}_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{0}} $$ Here, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda_{0}&=5000\text{Å }\\&=5000\times10^{-10}m\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}_{0}=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}}{5000\times10^{-10}} $$ $$ =4\times10^{-19}\mathrm{N} $$

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Introduction
summary: Cathode rays deflect in an electric field.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Negative electrons and neutral atoms collides and causes the ionization of gas enclosed in a discharge tube. - $$ \left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{electron}>>\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{proton} $$ $$ \left[\because\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{proton}=\frac{1}{1837}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_{electron}\right] $$ - Cathode rays deflect in an electric field.
##### Related Theory Stopping potential increases linearly with the frequency of incident photons but independent of the intensity.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Objective I
summary: The photoelectric effect (photoemission of electrons when light hits a metal) cannot be explained by classical wave theory — the effect depends on **frequency** (not intensity) of light, and has no time lag.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The photoelectric effect (photoemission of electrons when light hits a metal) cannot be explained by classical wave theory — the effect depends on **frequency** (not intensity) of light, and has no time lag. Einstein (1905) explained it by treating light as **photons** (particles of energy $E = h
u$). The photoelectric effect is direct evidence of the **particle nature** of light. Answer: (B).
- Einstein's photoelectric equation: $$KE_{m max} = h
u - \phi$$ where $h
u$ is the photon energy and $\phi = h
u_0$ is the work function (minimum energy to eject an electron). $KE_{m max}$ depends on **frequency** (not intensity) and is zero at the threshold frequency $
u_0 = \phi/h$. Increasing intensity increases the number of photoelectrons but not their maximum KE. Answer: (B).
- The **stopping potential** $V_s$ is the minimum reverse (negative) voltage applied to the anode that brings the photocurrent to zero. Even the fastest photoelectrons (with maximum KE) are stopped: $$eV_s = KE_{m max} = h
u - \phi$$ Measuring $V_s$ is the standard way to determine $KE_{m max}$ and hence test Einstein's equation. Answer: (B).
- De Broglie's relation: $\lambda = h/p$ where $p$ is momentum. For kinetic energy $K = p^2/(2m)$: $p = \sqrt{2mK}$. $$\lambda =  rac{h}{p} =  rac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}}$$ This describes the wave nature of matter — confirmed by electron diffraction experiments. Answer: (B).
- No matter how intense the light, if the photon energy $h
u < \phi$ (work function), no single photon has enough energy to eject an electron. Emission does not occur below the **threshold frequency** $
u_0 = \phi/h$. Low intensity only reduces the number of photoelectrons (not whether emission occurs). Answer: (B).

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Objective II
summary: Classical wave theory predicts: - Any frequency should work if intensity is high enough (wrong → A).
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Classical wave theory predicts: - Any frequency should work if intensity is high enough (wrong → A). - Energy accumulation would require a time lag (wrong → B). - Stopping potential should depend on intensity (wrong → C). The fact that photocurrent increases with intensity (D) is actually consistent with quantum theory too (more photons → more electrons), but it doesn't contradict classical theory. A, B, C are the hallmarks that classical theory fails to explain. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- De Broglie (1924) proposed that **all matter** has an associated wavelength $\lambda = h/p$. This has been confirmed experimentally for: - **Electrons** (Davisson–Germer diffraction experiment ✓) - **Protons** and **neutrons** (neutron diffraction used in crystallography ✓) - **Photons** (wave: interference/diffraction; particle: photoelectric effect ✓) Wave-particle duality is a universal quantum property. Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Overview
summary:
We know that
$$ h\mathsf{v}=\emptyset+\mathsf{K}\mathrm{E}_{\max} $$
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - We know that
$$ h\mathsf{v}=\emptyset+\mathsf{K}\mathrm{E}_{\max} $$
##### Related Theory Kinetic energy of photoelectron is, $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta K&=h\nu-h\nu_{0}=h c\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{_{0}}}\right)\\&=12400\left(\frac{1}{\lambda(\bar{\mathrm{A}})}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{_{0}}(\bar{\mathrm{A}})}\right)\end{aligned} $$ - According to de-Broglie wavelength $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p}\text{or}\lambda\propto\frac{1}{p} $$ The graph will be rectangular hyperbola.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
subtopic: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Particle Nature of Light: The Photon
summary: Energy $ E = h\nu $ velocity = c = v $ \lambda $ $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{\frac{h}{c}}{v}=\frac{h\nu}{c} $$ $$ \mathrm{E_{e x d.}=0}, $$ So momentum is conserved and energy is also conserved.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Energy $ E = h\nu $ velocity = c = v $ \lambda $ $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{\frac{h}{c}}{v}=\frac{h\nu}{c} $$ $$ \mathrm{E_{e x d.}=0}, $$ So momentum is conserved and energy is also conserved.
##### Related Theory Photons are chargeless massless particles that carry momentum $p = \frac{\hbar v}{c}$, where $\mathbf{v}$ is the frequency of light and $c$ is the speed of light. - Given, $$ \begin{aligned}&\lambda=600nm\\&\lambda=600\times10^{-9}m\\&\lambda=6\times10^{-7}m\\&P=3.3\times10^{-3}watt\\ \end{aligned} $$ We know power, $P = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{nhv}{t}$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{h\mathrm{v}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}\lambda}{h c} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=\frac{3.3\times10^{-3}\times6\times10^{-7}}{6.6\times10^{-34}\times3\times10^{8}} $$ $$ \frac{n}{t}=10^{16} $$ - We can write, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}_{a v}&=\frac{\Delta\mathrm{P}}{\Delta t}\\&=\frac{2\mathrm{I}}{c}\times\mathrm{A}\\&=\frac{2\times25\times10^{4}}{3\times10^{8}}\times15\times10^{-4}\\&=\frac{10\times25\times10^{4}\times10^{-4}}{10^{8}}\\&=2.5\times10^{-6}\mathrm{N}\end{aligned} $$ - Using the formula, $ P = \frac{N}{t} \times \frac{hc}{\lambda} $ $ = 200 \times 0.25 $ $ \frac{N}{t} = 50 \times \frac{\lambda}{hc} $ $ = \frac{50 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} $ $ = 1.5 \times 10^{20} $ - Number of photons emitted per second $$ \begin{aligned}&(1)\text{Number of photons cimbs:}\\&\quad n=\frac{P}{\left(\frac{\hbar c}{\lambda}\right)}\\&\quad\Rightarrow\quad P=\frac{\hbar c\lambda}{\lambda}\\&\quad\Rightarrow\quad\frac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}=\frac{n_{2}\lambda_{1}}{n_{1}\lambda_{2}}=\frac{1.02\times10^{15}\times5000}{10^{15}\times5100}\\&\quad\quad=1\end{aligned} $$ - Energy of photon, $ E = 1 \, MeV $ Momentum of photon, $ p = \frac{E}{c} $ $$ \begin{aligned}p&=\frac{E}{c}=\frac{1\times10^{6}\times1.6\times10^{-19}}{3\times10^{8}}\\&=5\times10^{-22}kg m/s\end{aligned} $$ - The value of planck's constant $$ h=6.63\times10^{-34}J s $$ - We have, The energy of light E = $ \frac{hc}{\lambda} $ The energy of light $ E = mc^{2} $ From (i) and (ii), we get $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{hc}{\lambda}=mc^{2}\\\frac{h}{\lambda}=mc=Momentum\end{array} $$ Thus, the momentum of the photon = $ \frac{h}{\lambda} $ - We have, $$ E=\frac{h c}{\lambda} $$ or $ E\lambda = hc = \text{constant} $ Therefore, $ E_{1}\lambda_{1}=E_{2}\lambda_{2} $ $$ 1\times10^{3}eV\times1.24\times10^{-9}=1\times10^{6}eV\times\lambda_{2} $$ Now, We have, $$ \lambda=1.24\times10^{-12} $$ SO. $$ \mathsf{v}=\frac{c}{\lambda} $$ $$ v=2.4\times10^{20}Hz $$

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: Wave Nature of Matter
summary: Given, $ Wavelength = \lambda $ $$ \boldsymbol{W}=0 $$ $$ mass=m_{e} $$ de-Broglie wavelength = $ \lambda_{d} $ We know from Einstein's relation Since $$…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- To find the ratio between $\lambda_{e}$ to $\lambda_{\alpha}$ $$ \begin{aligned}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}&=\frac{\frac{n}{\sqrt{2m_{e}(2e)\mathrm{V}}}}{\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}e\mathrm{V}}}}&\\&=\frac{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}e\mathrm{V}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}e\mathrm{V}}}\\ &=\frac{\sqrt{2(4m_{p})e\mathrm{V}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}e\mathrm{V}}}\\ &=2\sqrt{2}\frac{\sqrt{m_{p}}}{\sqrt{m_{e}}}\\ &=2\sqrt{2}\times42.8\quad\left[\because\sqrt{\frac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}}=42.8\right]\\ &=121\\ \lambda_{e}&=121\lambda_{\alpha}\\ \lambda_{e}&>\lambda_{\alpha}\\ \end{aligned} $$
- Given, $ Wavelength = \lambda $ $$ \boldsymbol{W}=0 $$ $$ mass=m_{e} $$ de-Broglie wavelength = $ \lambda_{d} $ We know from Einstein's relation Since $$ \begin{aligned}&h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\&\mathbf{W}=\mathbf{0}\\&h\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}\\ \end{aligned} $$ From de-Broglie hypothesis we know, $$ \lambda_{d}=\frac{h}{p} $$ and $$ p=\sqrt{2m K E} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \lambda_{d}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mKE}} $$ ∴From eq. (ii), $$ \lambda_{d}^{2}=\frac{h^{2}}{2mKE} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet $$ $$ KE=\frac{h^{2}}{2m\lambda_{d}^{2}} $$ From eq. (i), $$ \frac{h c}{\lambda}=KE $$ ∴ $$ \lambda=\frac{hc}{KE} $$ From eq. (iii) and (iv), $$ \lambda=\frac{hc}{h^{2}}\times2m\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{c}{h}2m\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ $$ \lambda=\left(\frac{2mc}{h}\right)\lambda_{d}^{2} $$ ##### △! Caution The de-broglie wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum $ \lambda \propto \frac{1}{p} $. Some students may assume a direct relationship or overlook the inverse proportionality.
- Kinetic energy of electron $$ \mathrm{K E=144~e V} $$ Means $$ \mathrm{eV}=144~\mathrm{eV} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}=144~\mathrm{V} $$ ∵ de-Broglie wavelength. From eq. (i), $$ \lambda=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{\mathbf{V}}}\mathring{\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{12.27}{\sqrt{144}}\mathring{A}\\&=\frac{12.27}{12}\mathring{A}=1.02\mathring{A}\\&=102\times10^{-3}nm.\end{aligned} $$
- The de-Broglie wavelength of any moving particle associated with momentum, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ where, $h = \mathrm{planck's}$ constant Now $$ K.E.=\frac{1}{2}m v^{2} $$ $$ K.E.=\frac{1}{2m}(mv)^{2}=\frac{p^{2}}{2m} $$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ p=\sqrt{2mKE} $$ ∴ From (i) and (ii), $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mKE}} $$ For Proton. $$ \lambda_{P}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{P}KE_{P}}} $$ For $ \alpha $-particle $$ \lambda_{\alpha}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}\mathrm{KE}_{\alpha}}} $$ From (iii) and (iv), $$ \frac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}kE_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{2m_{p}kE_{p}}} $$ $$\{\because\mathrm{~S i n c e~K E}_{\alpha}=\mathrm{K E}_{\mathrm{P}}\mathrm{~a n d~}m_{\alpha}=4m_{p}\}$$ $$ \frac{\lambda_{p}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{4m_{p}}{m_{p}}}=\frac{2}{1} $$ $$ \lambda_{\mathbf{P}}:\lambda_{\alpha}=2:1 $$
- From question $$ \mathbf{v}=\mathbf{v}_{0}\hat{i}\quad\mathrm{a n d}\quad\mathbf{E}=-\mathbf{E}_{0}\hat{i} $$ Now initial de-Broglic wavelength is, $$ \lambda_{0}=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}_{0}} $$ Acceleration of electron. $$ a=\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m} $$ Velocity after $t$ is, $$ \mathbf{v}=\left(\mathbf{v}_{0}+\frac{e\mathbf{E}_{0}}{m}t\right) $$ [This is obtained from, $ v = u + at $] So, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}}=\frac{h}{m\left(\mathrm{v}_{0}+\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m}t\right)} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{mv_{0}\left(1+\frac{eE_{0}}{mv_{0}}t\right)}=\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\left(1+\frac{eE_{0}}{mv_{0}}t\right)} $$ Thus $$ \lambda=\frac{\lambda_{0}}{\left(1+\frac{e\mathrm{E}_{0}}{m\mathrm{v}_{0}}t\right)} $$
- de-Broglie wavelength $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m(KE)}} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2m\left(\frac{3}{2}\mathrm{KT}\right)}} $$ $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{3mKT}} $$
- From de-Broglie equation $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ $$ p=\frac{h}{\lambda} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{p^{2}}{2m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2m\lambda^{2}} $$ $$ \therefore $$ $$ Also~in~X-ray,\lambda_{0}=\frac{hc}{E} $$ $$ \lambda_{0}=\frac{2mc\lambda^{2}}{h} $$
- For electron, $$ \lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}} $$ for photon. $$ \lambda_{ph}=\frac{hc}{E} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{ph}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}}\times\frac{\mathrm{E}}{hc}\\=\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}}{2m}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\times\frac{1}{c}\end{array} $$
- Energy of photon, (E) = $ \frac{12400}{5000} $ $ = 2.48 \, eV $ Work function, W = 2.28 eV Using Einstein's photoelectric equation $$ \mathbf{E}=\mathbf{W}+\mathbf{K}\mathbf{E}_{max} $$ $$ 2.48=2.28+(\mathrm{K.E.})_{\max} $$ $$ \therefore\quad(K.E.)_{\max}=0.20\ eV $$ for electron. $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mE}} $$ ⇔ $$ \lambda=28\textup{\AA} $$ So $$ \lambda\geq2.8\times10^{-9}\mathrm{m} $$
- Using de-Broglie hypothesis, $$ \lambda=\frac{h}{p} $$ Hence, $$ \lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2mK}} $$ and $$ \begin{array}{l}\lambda_{2}=\frac{h}{2m\times16K}\\=\frac{h}{4\sqrt{2mK}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{4}\end{array} $$ Using eq. (i) ∴ $$ \lambda_{2}=\frac{1}{4}\times\lambda_{1}=25\%of\lambda_{1} $$ % Change in wavelength $$ \begin{aligned}&=\frac{\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\times100=1-\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\times100\\&=75\%\end{aligned} $$ Thus, 75% changes in the wavelength.
- Using de-Broglie wavelength, $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{\sqrt[n]{2m\mathrm{K_{B}}\mathrm{T}}}{\quad}\\ \lambda&=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{0.83\times10^{-2}\times2\times1.6\times10^{-19}\times0.25}\\ &=\frac{3.08\times10^{-34}\times10^{25}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}}}\mathrm{m}=\frac{30.8}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}}}\mathring{A}\end{aligned} $$
- From de-Broglie hypothesis $$ \left|\frac{\Delta p}{p}\right|=\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} $$ $$ \frac{\Delta p}{p_{i}}=\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} $$ $$ p_{i}=\frac{p}{\underline{0.5}} $$ $$ p_{i}=\frac{1000}{5}p=200p $$
- We know that, $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{m\mathrm{v}}{q\mathrm{B}}\text{and}\lambda=\frac{h}{m\mathrm{v}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\lambda&=\frac{h}{mv}=\frac{h}{RqB}\\\lambda&=\frac{6.63\times10^{-34}}{0.83\times10^{-2}\times2\times1.6\times10^{-19}\times0.25}\\&=0.01\mathring{A}\end{aligned} $$
- We know that, $ \lambda = \frac{12.27}{\sqrt{\mathrm{v}}} $ $$ \begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow&\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{V}_{1}}}\\ \Rightarrow&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{V}_{2}}\\\therefore&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{25}{100}}\\\Rightarrow&\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{1}\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2}\end{array} $$

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
unitLabel: Unit VIII — Dual Nature of Radiation
title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Photocurrent $\propto$ intensity (power), so $I$ falls linearly. Stopping potential depends only on the photon frequency, which is unchanged, so $|V|$ stays constant.
- $\lambda_1<\lambda\Rightarrow$ no emission, $V_1=0$. Stopping potential grows with threshold wavelength, and $\lambda_3\gg\lambda_2$, so $V_3>V_2$, i.e. $V_2<V_3.$
- $p_e=\sqrt{2mE}$, $p_{ph}=E/c\Rightarrow\dfrac{p_e}{p_{ph}}=c\sqrt{\dfrac{2m}{E}}=3\times10^8\sqrt{\dfrac{2(9\times10^{-31})}{3.2\times10^{-18}}}\approx225.$
